items.title: Make GPT-5 mini the default coding workhorse
items.why: A cheap high-skill default model lets teams save the expensive model for cases where verification says more horsepower is actually needed.
how_to_steps: Set gpt-5-mini as the default model for routine implementation, repo Q and A, and PR-sized coding tasks.
Keep the loop verification-based so you escalate only when tests fail, tool use gets messy, or the task stalls.
Use lower verbosity for tight edit loops and increase it only when you need fuller plans or explanations.
Track solve rate and spend side by side for mini-default versus full-model escalations.
items.evidence: OpenAI reports GPT-5 mini scores 71.0% on SWE-bench Verified and 71.6% on Aider polyglot while costing $0.25 per 1M input tokens and $2 per 1M output tokens.
items.try_monday: Change one editor or agent default to GPT-5 mini and keep a simple escalation rule when verification fails.

items.title: Cache stable repo context instead of resending it every turn
items.why: Repeated codebase context is expensive and slow; caching it turns long-running coding loops into faster, cheaper interactions.
how_to_steps: Put stable prompt material first, such as system policy, coding standards, tool schema, and repo summary.
Reuse that exact prefix across turns and agent tool loops, appending only the new task delta at the end.
Apply caching first to codebase Q and A, autocomplete, and multi-turn debugging sessions that resend long context.
Measure time to first token and input-token cost before and after the change.
items.evidence: Anthropic says prompt caching cuts cost by up to 90% and latency by up to 85%; one 100k-token prompt dropped from 11.5s to 2.4s time-to-first-token.
items.try_monday: Cache the stable repo and policy prefix in one high-volume coding assistant and compare TTFT and spend over a day.
